title: Classical ML & Forecasting in Practice
pitchHtml: This is the module that pays the bills. Strip away the hype and most of the realised value in AI today still comes from a boring, reliable engine: a few good features feeding a gradient-boosted tree, or a well-chosen forecast. These methods are cheap to build, cheap to run, explainable enough to trust, and beat fancy deep learning on the structured (tabular) and time-series problems that make up the bulk of business demand. Your job as a tech lead is not to derive them — it's to know which one a problem wants, what it costs, how to scope a mission around it, and when even this is too much. The recurring lesson of this module: good features + boosted trees beat deep learning on most tabular problems, at a fraction of the cost — and forecasting rewards the team that respects the value/complexity budget.

items.html: Frame a problem as supervised (regression vs classification) or unsupervised, and walk the "start simple" ladder before committing
Decide when linear/logistic regression is the right answer — and when it's "just" the baseline that everything else must beat
Explain why gradient-boosted trees are the default winner for tabular data, and route between Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM and CatBoost
Tell the difference between clustering and dimensionality reduction, and recognise when segmentation actually helps a client
Choose a forecasting approach (classical baseline vs Prophet vs ML vs deep learning) and know why classical methods so often win
Insist on backtesting / walk-forward validation for any time-series promise, and on ranges, not points, for any forecast
Scope and price a churn, forecasting, segmentation, or anomaly-scoring mission with a named deliverable and a buyer

subsections.title: Framing: Supervised vs Unsupervised, and the "Start Simple" Ladder

blocks.html:
Before any algorithm, settle two questions: do we have labels? and what's the cheapest thing that could work?

Supervised = you have examples with the answer attached (past customers tagged churned/retained; past sales with the actual number). The model learns the mapping from features to that answer. Two flavours:

  • Regression — predict a number (next month's demand, a price, a lifetime value).
  • Classification — predict a category (will churn / won't; fraud / legit; which of five tiers).

Unsupervised = no answer column. The model finds structure: groups that resemble each other (clustering), or a compressed view of the data (dimensionality reduction). Useful for exploration and segmentation, but it can't tell you whether a grouping is the right one for the business — that judgement stays with you.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: "No labels" is to supervised ML what "no data" is to AI overall — a stop condition you must detect early. If the outcome you want to predict was never recorded, the first mission isn't modelling; it's defining and capturing the label. A churn model needs a clean, agreed definition of "churned" before a single feature is built.
The start-simple ladder — climb only when the rung below provably fails:

RungTry thisMove up only when
0A rule / SQL / a spreadsheet heuristicThe logic is genuinely fuzzy, not just unwritten
1Logistic / linear regression (the baseline)The baseline's accuracy leaves real money on the table
2Gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost)— usually the right stopping point for tabular data
3Deep learningData is unstructured (text, images, audio) or truly massive
Most tabular missions should land on rung 2. A surprising number are fine at rung 0 or 1. Make stakeholders justify each step up.

subsections.title: Linear & Logistic Regression — the Honest Baseline

blocks.html:
These are the simplest learnable models, and that simplicity is a feature, not a weakness.

  • Linear regression predicts a number as a weighted sum of inputs. Each weight is an interpretable "all else equal, +1 here moves the prediction by this much."
y^=β0+β1x1+β2x2+⋯+βkxk\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_k x_ky^​=β0​+β1​x1​+β2​x2​+⋯+βk​xk​

where y^\hat{y}y^​ is the prediction, each xix_ixi​ is an input feature, each βi\beta_iβi​ is the learned weight on that feature, and β0\beta_0β0​ is the intercept (the baseline prediction when all features are zero). The model is fit by minimising the mean squared error (MSE) between predictions and truth:
MSE=1n∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2MSE=n1​i=1∑n​(yi​−y^​i​)2

with nnn the number of training rows, yiy_iyi​ the true value, and y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​ the predicted value. Squaring makes all errors positive and penalises big misses disproportionately. Use case: any "predict a number" problem where you also want to read off the drivers — a regulator or a CFO can be told "each extra support ticket adds 2 points of churn risk."

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The coefficients are the explanation. Because the output is a plain sum, βi\beta_iβi​ literally answers "how much does the prediction move per unit of this feature, holding the rest fixed?" — which is exactly the sentence a non-technical stakeholder needs. That readability is the whole reason this model survives in regulated settings.

• Logistic regression does the same but squashes the output into a probability for classification ("73% likely to churn"). Despite the name, it's a classifier. It takes the same linear combination z=β0+β1x1+⋯+βkxkz = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_k x_kz=β0​+β1​x1​+⋯+βk​xk​ and passes it through the sigmoid function:
p=σ(z)=11+e−zp = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}p=σ(z)=1+e−z1​
where zzz is the raw linear score and ppp is the output probability. Use case: any yes/no prediction you want as a calibrated likelihood you can threshold or rank by (churn / not, fraud / legit).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The sigmoid is an S-shaped squashing function: it maps any real score — from −∞-\infty−∞ to +∞+\infty+∞ — into the open interval (0,1)(0,1)(0,1), so the output always reads as a probability. A very negative zzz flattens toward 0, a very positive zzz saturates toward 1, and z=0z = 0z=0 gives exactly 0.5 (the decision boundary). That's what turns an unbounded linear score into "73% likely."

Why a tech lead should love them:

  • Interpretability. You can read the coefficients to a regulator, a doctor, or a credit committee. When the explanation is part of the deliverable, this matters more than a point of accuracy.
  • Cheap and fast. Trains in seconds, runs in microseconds, costs essentially nothing to serve.
  • A baseline that disciplines the project. Every fancier model must beat the regression by enough to justify its cost. If XGBoost adds 0.5% over logistic regression on a problem where 0.5% is noise, you've learned something valuable: ship the simple one.

When they're genuinely the right answer (not just the baseline): relationships are roughly linear/monotonic, you have few features, the dataset is small, or explainability is a hard requirement (regulated credit, insurance, clinical, anything you must defend). Their weakness — they don't capture interactions and non-linearities on their own — is exactly what trees fix.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Think of logistic regression as drawing a single straight dividing line (or flat plane) through the data. If the two classes can be roughly separated by a straight line, it's elegant and bulletproof. If the boundary is wiggly — "churn risk spikes for new customers on expensive plans but not either alone" — a straight line can't bend to fit it, and you want trees.

subsections.title: Tree-Based Models: the Workhorse of Tabular ML

blocks.html:
A decision tree is a flowchart the machine learns: "is tenure < 3 months? → is plan = premium? → predict churn." Intuitive, but a single deep tree memorises the training data (overfits) and is unstable — change a few rows and the tree redraws itself. The breakthroughs are two ways of combining many trees so their errors cancel.

How a tree picks each split. At every node the algorithm chooses the question that makes the resulting groups as pure (single-class) as possible. Two standard measures of "mixedness," where pkp_kpk​ is the fraction of items in class kkk at that node:
G=1−∑kpk2H=−∑kpklog⁡2pkG = 1 - \sum_{k} p_k^2 \qquad\qquad H = -\sum_{k} p_k \log_2 p_kG=1−k∑​pk2​H=−k∑​pk​log2​pk​

GGG is Gini impurity and HHH is entropy. Both are 0 when a node is pure (one class) and maximal when classes are evenly mixed. The split chosen is the one with the largest information gain — the drop in impurity from parent to the weighted average of its children:

Information Gain=Impurityparent−∑childrennchildnparent Impuritychild\text{Information Gain} = \text{Impurity}_{\text{parent}} - \sum_{\text{children}} \frac{n_{\text{child}}}{n_{\text{parent}}}\,\text{Impurity}_{\text{child}}Information Gain=Impurityparent​−children∑​nparent​nchild​​Impuritychild​
Use case: this is the engine inside every tree and forest — it decides which feature, at which threshold becomes the next question.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Both GGG and HHH measure how "mixed" a basket of labels is: a basket that's all one class scores 0, a 50/50 basket scores highest. A split is good when it sorts the mixed basket into purer ones, so the tree greedily picks whichever question buys the biggest purity gain. Gini and entropy almost always agree on the choice; Gini is cheaper to compute, so it's the common default.

Bagging → Random Forest (cut variance). Train hundreds of trees, each on a random slice of the data and features, then average their votes. Individual trees are erratic; their average is stable. Robust, hard to misuse, a great strong baseline.

Boosting → Gradient-Boosted Trees (cut bias). Build trees in sequence, each new one focused on correcting the errors the previous ones still make. The ensemble is an additive model built one tree at a time:
Fm(x)=Fm−1(x)+γ hm(x)F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \gamma\, h_m(x)Fm​(x)=Fm−1​(x)+γhm​(x)

where Fm(x)F_m(x)Fm​(x) is the ensemble's prediction after mmm trees, Fm−1(x)F_{m-1}(x)Fm−1​(x) is the previous ensemble, hm(x)h_m(x)hm​(x) is the new tree trained to fit the residual errors still left over, and γ\gammaγ is the learning rate (a small step size, e.g. 0.1, that keeps each correction modest). The ensemble climbs steadily toward the signal. This is the technique behind XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost — and the reason it dominates tabular ML.

blocks.title: bagging vs boosting in one line
blocks.html: Bagging is a crowd voting independently so individual mistakes wash out — it reduces variance (instability). Boosting is a relay team where each runner is told exactly where the last one fell short — it reduces bias (systematic error): each tree hmh_mhm​ targets what the running total Fm−1F_{m-1}Fm−1​ still gets wrong, so the sum keeps shrinking the systematic error. Bagging = "average away the noise," boosting = "relentlessly fix the residual mistake."
Why gradient-boosted trees are the default winner for tabular data:

PropertyWhy it wins on real business data
Captures interactions & non-linearitiesAutomatically, without you hand-specifying them
Mixed feature typesNumbers + categories together; CatBoost handles categoricals natively
Robust to scale & outliersNo need to normalise; splits don't care about units
Tolerates missing valuesOften handled internally, not a blocker
Strong out-of-the-boxGood results before heavy tuning — fast to a working model
Cheap to train & serveMinutes on a laptop/CPU; milliseconds to predict; no GPU
Explainable enoughFeature importance + SHAP values give per-prediction reasons
Routing between the popular libraries:

LibraryReach for it whenNote
Random ForestYou want a robust baseline with almost no tuningHard to break; rarely the best but rarely bad
XGBoostThe proven default; mature ecosystem, huge communityThe safe institutional choice
LightGBMLarge datasets, you care about training speedFastest on big tabular data; sensible default for many teams
CatBoostLots of high-cardinality categorical featuresBest categorical handling; strong defaults out of the box

For a leader, the honest summary: pick any one of XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost, tune it modestly, and you have a model that will beat a deep network on nearly any tabular problem — for a tiny fraction of the cost, talent, and risk. That single fact reshapes a lot of roadmaps.

subsections.title: A Quick Worked Example: Churn Propensity

blocks.html:
A telco wants to know which subscribers to call this month to prevent cancellation. Walk the ladder:

  1. Label. Agree the definition: "churned = no active line 30 days after contract end." Pull 24 months of history; tag each subscriber-month churned / retained. (This is most of the work — see Working with Data: Sources, Quality & Feature Engineering.)
  2. Features. Tenure, plan, monthly spend, recent usage trend, support tickets, payment failures, days-to-contract-end.
  3. Baseline. Logistic regression. Result: it ranks customers by risk and flags the obvious signals (payment failures, usage collapse). Already useful, already explainable.
  4. Escalate. XGBoost on the same features. It discovers interactions the line missed — e.g. risk spikes for high-spend customers whose usage dropped in the last 30 days — and ranks the at-risk list more sharply.
  5. Decide and deliver. The deliverable is not "a model." It's a monthly ranked call-list plus, per customer, the top reasons (via SHAP) so retention agents know what to offer. Success is measured against a held-out period and ideally an A/B test of "called vs not" — squarely a Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation concern.

Notice what carried the value: a clean label, sensible features, and a boosted tree. No deep learning in sight.

When there's no label, two tools find structure. Useful, but easy to oversell — there's no "accuracy" to point at, so the bar is business usefulness, judged by you.

Clustering — grouping similar things. k-means is the workhorse: you tell it how many groups (k) to find, and it partitions the data into k blobs around their centres. Classic use: customer segmentation ("here are five behavioural segments; marketing can treat them differently"). The catch: clusters are only as meaningful as the features and the chosen k, and the algorithm will always return groups — even in noise. Segmentation helps when (a) you'll act differently per group, and (b) a human can name the groups sensibly.

Dimensionality reduction — compressing many columns into a few.

  • PCA = "find the handful of directions along which the data actually varies, and describe each point by those." Compresses, de-noises, and speeds up downstream models; the trade-off is the new axes aren't directly human-readable.
  • t-SNE / UMAP = primarily visualisation — squashing high-dimensional data onto a 2-D picture so you can see clusters. Great for an exploratory chart in a report; do not read distances or cluster sizes literally — they're qualitative maps, not measurements.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: PCA is lossy compression for tables: keep the few directions that carry most of the variation, drop the rest as noise. t-SNE/UMAP are the "photograph" you show a stakeholder so they can see that segments exist — a communication tool, not an analysis you'd staple a decision to.

Forecasting is its own discipline because time breaks the usual assumptions — rows aren't independent, the future leaks into the past if you're careless, and "accuracy on past data" can be a mirage. It's also one of the most sellable capabilities (demand, sales, cash, capacity, headcount), so it's worth real space.

The essentials to carry into any forecasting conversation:

  • Most series decompose into trend + seasonality + noise, and most methods just estimate the first two and extrapolate. Start with a seasonal-naïve baseline ("this December ≈ last December") — every model must beat it — then exponential smoothing (ETS) / ARIMA / Prophet; reach for gradient-boosted trees on lag & calendar features only with many series or external drivers, and deep-learning forecasters only above that. (The M-competition bake-offs keep finding that simple methods win on most real series — don't open with a Transformer for "forecast our monthly sales.")
  • Backtesting / walk-forward validation is non-negotiable. Never evaluate a forecaster with a random train/test split — shuffling lets it peek at the future and the score becomes fiction. Train on the past, predict the next window, roll forward, repeat — exactly how it will be used (mechanics in Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation).
  • Ranges beat points. "80% chance between 850 and 1,200" lets a client set safety stock or size a buffer; a bare "1,000" sold as certainty is how you lose trust when reality lands outside it.

blocks.title: This is the short version
blocks.html: For the dedicated treatment — classical methods with formulas (ETS, ARIMA), ML and 2026 foundation-model forecasting, the backtesting mechanics, and the metric zoo (MAPE / sMAPE / MASE) — see Time Series & Forecasting in Practice.

These four archetypes cover the bulk of classical-ML and forecasting demand. Each is genuinely sellable as a fixed-scope engagement.

  • Churn / Propensity / Lead-Scoring Model — Problem: "we waste retention/sales effort on the wrong customers." Deliverable: a scored, ranked monthly list (e.g. top-N customers to call) with per-record reason codes, delivered to the CRM/BI tool the team already uses. Scope: 6-10 weeks, most of it data and label work. Who buys: CMO, Head of Retention, VP Sales, RevOps.

  • Demand / Sales Forecasting — Problem: stock-outs and overstock, or blind capacity/cash planning. Deliverable: a forecasting service producing weekly forecasts with uncertainty ranges, backtested against history, feeding a planning dashboard; per-SKU or per-region as scoped. Scope: 8-12 weeks. Who buys: Supply Chain / Operations Director, Head of Planning, CFO (for cash/headcount).

  • Customer Segmentation — Problem: "we treat all customers the same." Deliverable: a named, profiled set of segments (with a clear "how to act on each" playbook) plus a repeatable scoring rule to assign new customers. Scope: 4-6 weeks. Who buys: CMO, Head of CRM, product/growth leads. (Lower-risk, faster, and a good door-opener — but only sell it if the client will actually act differently per segment.)

  • Predictive Maintenance / Anomaly Scoring — Problem: equipment fails unexpectedly, or "something weird is happening and we find out too late." Deliverable: an anomaly/failure-risk score per asset (or per transaction) with alerting thresholds, tuned so the alert volume is something the team can actually action. Scope: 8-14 weeks (label scarcity makes this longer). Who buys: Head of Maintenance/Reliability, Plant Manager, or Fraud/Risk lead for the transaction variant.

Buy-vs-build: the model is cheap and commodity (open-source libraries) — your billable value is the data work, the label definition, the feature engineering, the honest validation, and the integration into the client's workflow. Don't sell "an XGBoost model"; sell the decision it powers, wired into where they work. What to charge for: the end-to-end engagement, and a separate monitoring/retraining retainer (models drift — that recurring revenue is real and justified). What NOT to promise: a specific accuracy number before you've seen the data; a point forecast as if it were certain; "set and forget" (every model decays). Commit to a method for finding and proving the number, not to the number.
As of 2026 — treat names/versions as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
scikit-learnLinear/logistic, RF, k-means, PCA, baselinesOSSThe default toolbox; everything starts here
XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoostGradient-boosted trees (the tabular workhorse)OSSPick one; all three are excellent
statsmodelsARIMA, ETS, classical statsOSSMature classical time-series
Prophet (Meta)Analyst-friendly forecasting w/ seasonality & holidaysOSSFast to a decent daily forecast; not a silver bullet
StatsForecast / NeuralForecast (Nixtla)Fast classical + modern forecasting at scaleOSSStrong for many-series forecasting
SHAPPer-prediction explanations (reason codes)OSSTurns a tree into something you can defend
AutoML (auto-sklearn, FLAML, H2O, cloud AutoML)Fast first model / non-specialist teamsMixedGreat for a baseline; not a substitute for judgment
Cloud ML platforms (SageMaker, Vertex, Azure ML)Managed training/serving/monitoringPaidConvenient; watch lock-in & cost — see MLOps & Productionization
For deeper production patterns (serving, monitoring, retraining) this table only points the way — that's the MLOps module's territory.

SituationClassical ML is overkill — do this instead
The rule is known and stable ("flag orders > €10k")Write the SQL/rule; ship in days, explain in one line
You have a few dozen historical data pointsNo model will generalise; use a heuristic or expert judgment
No agreed definition of the outcome to predictDefine and capture the label first — that's the real mission
"Forecast next month" with two years of flat monthly dataSeasonal-naïve baseline; a fancy model won't beat "same as last year" much
The decision is made twice a year by a personGive them a dashboard; don't automate a near-zero-volume call
Stakeholder wants deep learning for tabular dataBoosted trees first — cheaper, faster, usually better; escalate only on evidence
You'd spend more on the model than the problem is worthRe-read the value/complexity budget; sometimes "good enough by hand" wins

The senior move is the same as everywhere in this path: prove the simple thing fails before reaching for the complex one. On tabular and time-series problems, the simple thing wins far more often than enthusiasm expects.

• Aurélien Géron — Hands-On Machine Learning — the practical reference for everything in §2-§5; intuition-first. (Paid)
  • Hyndman & Athanasopoulos — Forecasting: Principles and Practice (free online, "FPP3") — the standard, clear, low-maths forecasting text. Read the trend/seasonality and backtesting chapters.
  • Provost & Fawcett — Data Science for Business — frames these methods in commercial terms. (Paid)

• scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost and Prophet official docs/tutorials — the fastest path to a working model.
  • The M-competition retrospectives (Makridakis et al.) — the evidence behind "classical often beats complex." Skim the conclusions.

• Deeper math → Supervised Learning: Trees, Ensembles & Boosting, Supervised Learning: Linear Models & Kernel Methods, Unsupervised Learning, and Time Series Analysis & Forecasting (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code → Classical ML Mastery (ML Engineer Path) — production-grade training, tuning, and serving of these models.

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Which model does this problem want?"

items.html: Is the target a number (regression) or a category (classification) — or is there no label (unsupervised)?
Is the data tabular? If yes, default to boosted trees and make anything fancier earn its place.
Have we run a baseline (logistic/linear, or seasonal-naïve for time series) for everything to beat?
Is explainability a hard requirement? If yes, favour linear models or add SHAP to the tree.
For time series: are we using walk-forward / backtesting, not a random split?
For forecasts: are we delivering a range, not just a point?
Is the deliverable wired into the client's workflow/tool, not just a notebook?

blocks.html:
• "Let's use deep learning" for a few thousand rows of tabular data. (Boosted trees, almost always.)
  • A forecast validated with a random train/test split. (It peeked at the future; the score is fiction.)
  • A point forecast presented as certainty. (Reality will land outside it, and trust dies.)
  • Clustering with no plan to act differently per group. (Pretty chart, zero value.)
  • Reading distances/sizes off a t-SNE/UMAP plot as if they were measurements.
  • No agreed label definition before feature work starts. (You're building on sand.)
  • "We'll deploy once and we're done." (Models drift — budget monitoring & retraining.)

• "What decision changes once this prediction/forecast exists, and what is that decision worth?"
  • "How is the outcome we want to predict recorded today, and do we agree on its definition?"
  • "How far ahead, how often, and at what grain do you need the forecast (daily? per SKU? per store)?"
  • "Can you act on a range, or does a process force you to commit to one number — and how do you handle being wrong today?"
  • "Does the output need to be explainable to a regulator, a customer, or an auditor?"
  • "Where will this score/forecast appear so someone actually uses it?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Classify any incoming problem as regression / classification / unsupervised, and place it on the start-simple ladder
Explain to a client why gradient-boosted trees are the tabular default — and when a linear model is the better sell
Distinguish bagging from boosting in one sentence each, without reaching for maths
Recommend a forecasting approach and justify not defaulting to deep learning (the M-competition lesson)
Insist on walk-forward backtesting and probabilistic ranges, and explain why points and random splits mislead
Scope a churn, forecasting, segmentation, or anomaly mission with a named deliverable, realistic duration, and the buyer
